coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive bioassay of epitope of Mucin-16 protein (CA 125) in human plasma samples using a novel immunoassay based on silver conductive nano-ink: A new platform in early stage diagnosis of ovarian cancer and efficient management
coredata.dc:creator: Jafari, Mohsen
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Solhi, Elham
Hassanpour, Soodabeh
Shadjou, Nasrin
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
Jouyban, Abolghasem
Mahboob, Soltanali
coredata.dc:description: Ovarian cancer (OC) is known to be one of the most lethal malignancies associated with women disease. The CA-125 protein is a repetitive epitope of MUC-16, which plays key role in enhancing the proliferation of cancer cells and inhibiting anticancer immune responses. It is the most widely used biomarker for early stage diagnosis of OC. Also it is the only serum marker which currently used in clinical diagnosis. Monitoring of CA-125 protein in the serum sample is also valuable in evaluating the response of ovarian cancer to treatment. In this research, a novel immunoassay based on immobilization of CA-125 antibody on the biointerface of silver nanoparticles modified graphene quantum dots ink (Ag NPs-GQDs) was successfully designed to recognition of CA-125 protein in a human plasma sample. The supplied immunoassay presents the proper ability to detect and determine the amount of CA-125 biomarker in low concentrations of CA-125 biomarker. The proposed immunosensor was employed for the detection of CA-125 using differential pulse voltammetry (DPVs) and square wave voltammetry (SWVs) techniques. The proposed interface leads to enhancement of accessible surface area for immobilizing a high amount of anti-CA-125 antibody, increasing electrical conductivity, boosting stability, catalytic properties and biocompatibility. Under the optimized operating conditions, the low limit of quantitation (LLOQ) for the proposed immunosensor was recorded as 0.01 U/mL, which this evaluation was performed at highly linear range of 0.01–400 U/mL. The proposed immunoassay was successfully applied for the monitoring of CA-125 in unprocessed human plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ovarian cancer
Mucin-16 protein
Bioassay
CA-125 biomarker
Nano-ink
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and conductivity study of inks
Synthesis of graphene quantum dots doped by silver nanoparticle (ag NPs-GQDs) conductive nano ink
Conductivity study of Ag NPs-GQDs ink
Characterization of synthesis Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink and fabricated immunosensor
Characterization of synthesis Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink
Sensitivity study of engineered immunosensor in various concentration of CA-125 biomarker in human plasma sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographic images of various patterns with different thicknesses and shapes obtained by Ag NPs-GQDs composite nano-ink on photo paper.
cyclic voltammograms for electrodeposition of Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink on the surface of GCE in the potential range of −1 to +1 V and scan rate of 100 mV/s Number of cycle was 30 cycles.
FE-SEM images from Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink in different magnitudes.
EDS result for Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink bulk sample.

CVs (a), DPVs (b), SWVs (c), in the potential range of −1 to +1 V. EIS (d) for Ag NPs-GQDs, Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab, Ag NPs-GQD/Ab/BSA, Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/BSA/Ag. Supporting electrolyte is 1 mM solution of Riboflavin.
a) DPVs responses of the immunosensor for different concentrations of CA 125: 15, 50, 100, 200,400 U/mL in 1 mM of riboflavin. b) Calibration curve.

DPVs Ag NPs-GQD/Ab/BSA/Ag/GCE in the presence of a) serum protein (BSA, PSA, P53, CA-15.3) and b) physiological molecules (glucose and uric acid) and related c) histograms. SWVs Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/BSA/Ag/GCE in presence of d) serum protein (BSA, PSA, P53, CA-15.3) and e) physiological molecules (glucose and uric acid) and related f) histograms. All evaluation carried on 1 mM riboflavin in potential range of −1 to +1 V. Furthermore, the utilized concentration of CA-125 antigen was 15 U/mL.
Synthesis process of Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink.
Fabrication procedure of the electrochemical immunosensor for detection of CA 125.
A comparison of the performance of several immunosensor for the detection of CA-125.

row.entry: Surface Plasmaon resonance capacitor immunosensors label free

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive bioassay of epitope of Mucin-16 protein (CA 125) in human plasma samples using a novel immunoassay based on silver conductive nano-ink: A new platform in early stage diagnosis of ovarian cancer and efficient management

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Higher Education Institute of Rab-Rashid, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Research Center, Uremia University, Uremia 57154, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ovarian cancer (OC) is known to be one of the most lethal malignancies associated with women disease. The CA-125 protein is a repetitive epitope of MUC-16, which plays key role in enhancing the proliferation of cancer cells and inhibiting anticancer immune responses. It is the most widely used biomarker for early stage diagnosis of OC. Also it is the only serum marker which currently used in clinical diagnosis. Monitoring of CA-125 protein in the serum sample is also valuable in evaluating the response of ovarian cancer to treatment. In this research, a novel immunoassay based on immobilization of CA-125 antibody on the biointerface of silver nanoparticles modified graphene quantum dots ink (Ag NPs-GQDs) was successfully designed to recognition of CA-125 protein in a human plasma sample. The supplied immunoassay presents the proper ability to detect and determine the amount of CA-125 biomarker in low concentrations of CA-125 biomarker. The proposed immunosensor was employed for the detection of CA-125 using differential pulse voltammetry (DPVs) and square wave voltammetry (SWVs) techniques. The proposed interface leads to enhancement of accessible surface area for immobilizing a high amount of anti-CA-125 antibody, increasing electrical conductivity, boosting stability, catalytic properties and biocompatibility. Under the optimized operating conditions, the low limit of quantitation (LLOQ) for the proposed immunosensor was recorded as 0.01 U/mL, which this evaluation was performed at highly linear range of 0.01–400 U/mL. The proposed immunoassay was successfully applied for the monitoring of CA-125 in unprocessed human plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ovarian cancer (OC) is known to be one of the most lethal malignancies associated with women disease, annually more than 230,000 new cases and 152,000 deaths is reported associated with it. OC accounts for approximately 4% of cases and 4.2% of deaths in the female population worldwide [1]. It is one of eighth common cancer among women and is the fourth leading cause of cancer deaths in developed countries. The highest rates of OC are seen in eastern and central European women population [2,3].

Over the past 50 years, with regard to the development of radical surgery methods and chemotherapeutic procedures, the survival rate among OC patients has not been remarkably improved [4], and screening studies have not been successful for the past 30 years. Studies show that in some cases, screened populations reveal a significant mortality rate that has been subject to unnecessary surgery as a result of false positive tests [5].

In spite of all treatments, the 5-year survival rate for patients with advanced OC levels (stages 3 and 4) is reported to be between 15% and 20%, in which the survival rate for early detection (stage 1) is more than 90% [6,7].

Current diagnostic methods include: physical examination, transvaginal ultrasonography (TVU), serum CA-125 biomarker level measurement, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and positron emission tomography (PET) [8–10]. OC is known as a “silent killer” due to the lack of reliable diagnostic tools and major diagnosis of the disease in the final stages [11].

Literature reviews show that there are no effective screening tools for early diagnosis of OC [12]. So the design, manufacture and development of reliable and non-invasive diagnostic tools is one of the necessary requirements for prevention and treatment of this disease.

Biomarkers can be defined as specific feature of any type of cells that can be measured and are considered as indicators in the evaluation and control of biological, pathogenic and therapeutically processes [13]. Biomarkers are classified based on their chemical nature to DNA, RNA, and proteins and categorized as prognostic and diagnostic according to level of the disease [14].

The CA-125 is a repetitive epitope of muc-16, which plays key role in enhancing the proliferation of cancer cells and inhibiting anticancer immune responses [15]. Biochemically, the CA-125 is O-linked type glycoprotein and its molecular weight is estimated to be 2.5 to 5 miloDaltons [16] [17]. It is the most widely used biomarker for diagnosing OC and is considered to be the golden standard that was first discovered by the Bast knapp in 1981 [18] [19].

Increased levels of CA-125 have been observed in 90% of patients with advanced level of OC and also in 50% of cases with early stage of disease [20,21]. This biomarker is widely used in early diagnosis and is the only serum marker which currently used in clinical diagnosis [22]. The normal value prescribed for healthy people is less than 35 U/mL [23]. Monitoring of CA-125 in the serum sample is also valuable in evaluating the response of ovarian cancer to treatment [24]. However, there are some limitations in using it. In some cases, the result of the false positive CA-125 test can lead to unnecessary treatment such as surgery for the patient, as well as false negative responses to patients in the early stages of the disease, may discontinue treatment and cause disease progression [25]. Therefore, it is necessary to use new methods with high sensitivity, precision and reliability in its diagnosis [26].

Biosensors are diagnostic tools that, in comparison to commercial analytical techniques, have some merits such as: high specificity and sensitivity, low cost, high performance, fast and portability, and used in a wide variety of fields such as medicine, Food industry, agriculture and environmental monitoring [27–29]. The biosensing system operates through the identification of a defined electrical signal through the conversion of biological data (usually the desired analyte concentration) [30].

An immunosensor is defined as a device that can detect the binding of the antibody-antigen and the formation of a sustained complex, provided that both components can be immobilized on the desired substrate. In this way, immunosensors have attracted a lot of attention due to intrinsic property of binding to their specific antigens and make high specificity [31].

The utility of appropriate nanomaterials in immunosensors is one of the important factors in improving the performance of electrochemical sensors. In addition, the use of different nanomaterials can be effective in improving the electrode capabilities, including stability, electrical conductivity and sensitivity [32,33].

One of the novel nano materials is graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The merits of GQDs include low cost, high solubility in a variety of solvents, and functionalization with various functional groups. The redox behavior has been proven in GQDs, which can be recorded by electrochemical techniques. In addition, GQDs-based electrochemical sensors have shown that this nanoparticle has a high potential for electron capture and donate compared to other carbon nanomaterials [34]. It is an important issue for the practical application of Ag NPs because of it is simple oxidation and aggregation. The use of stabilizers is an effective strategy to resolve this problem. Types of structure such as polymers, mineral molecules, silicones and carbon based materials are recommended to improve the stability of Ag NPs. GQDs hybridization with metal nanomaterials produces high potential for catalytic, optical and electron transfer systems application. In addition, GQDs hybridization with silver nanoparticles also increases the stability of silver nanoparticles. Silver nanoparticles can also increase sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability [35,36].

Graphene-base conductive inks are commercially utilized and widely used in the electronic industry for the manufacturing of lithium batteries, supercapacitors and sensors [37]. It reveals the desired properties including intrinsic chemical properties, environmental stability, and excellent electrical conductivity. For this reason, the use of electrodes modified with graphene based material present better electrocatalytic and electroactive performance compared to other carbon nanomaterials [38,39]. Also, low cost, high surface area, easy preparation and safety are the other advantages of it. In recent years, extend of using conductive inks with noble metal nanoparticle fillers have been developed [40].

Metal nanoparticles play an important role among the components of conductive inks. Silver has unique feature and the high capability of electrical conductivity at the scale of per unit in comparison to other metals [41,42]. The conductive ink which contains silver nano particles has prominent feature of other conductive inks [43]. A significant part of this prominence is related to the presence of silver nanoparticle (Ag NPs) [42]. Ag NPs has excellent optical, thermal and electrical properties and it has some advantage in compare to other metal, such as: low toxicity, biocompatibility and ability to support electrocatalytic activity [44–46].

To the best of our acknowledge, fabrication of the Ag NPs-GQDs conductive ink on the surface of electrode and its application in providing electrochemical immunosensors for CA-125 detection was initially monitored in this work. So application of prepared immunosensor based on conductive ink was investigated by some of electrochemical techniques (DPV, SWV, CV, and EIS). The application of conductive inks as a new matrix for providing excellent substrate to immobilize bio-recognition element (antibody) toward monitoring of CA-125 antigen in human plasma samples.

All chemicals used in this research are in an analytical grade. Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) K-30 (molecule weight ≈300 g), bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), ethanol, and ethylene glycol (EG), Riboflavin (RF), AgNO3, Glucose and Uric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada). Sodium hydroxide supplied from Merck (Germany). Nitric acid (HNO3) and Hydrochloric acid (HCL) and acetone were purchased from Scharlau chemie S.A. Human CA-125 ELISA Kit contain antibody (HRP Labeled anti-CA 125) standard, CA-125 antigen, dilute solutions and standard buffer, also CA 15–3 antigen and Prostate specific antigen (PSA) were purchased from AB Technology Co., (Gotenberg, Sweden). P53 antigen was bought from IBL International Co. (Germany).

Electrochemical experiments for voltammetric measurements were performed with a computer controlled AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands), driven with NOVA 1.11 software. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were accomplished in three-electrode cell (from Metrohm) powered by an electrochemical system consisting of PalmSens 4c system, electrochemical interface. The system was run on a laptop using PSTrace5 software. The ac voltage amplitude used was 10 mV and the equilibrium time was 5 s. Both electrochemical system were composed of conventional three-electrode cell was used with an Ag/AgCl (Methrom, The Netherlands) as a reference electrode, and a Pt wire was used as a counter-electrode. The working electrode was glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (d = 2 mm) (from Azar electrode Co., Iran). The surface morphology of the modified electrodes study and energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) for components element analysis was evaluated with a MIRA3 FEG-SEM (SEM, Hitachi Ltd., Czech,). GC electrode (2 mm in diameter) was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05 mm alumina slurry (Beuhler, USA) followed by rinsing thoroughly with double distilled water. Also the resistance of conductive nano-ink was evaluated by ohmmeter (XIOLE, XL830L, China, multimeter).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and conductivity study of inks
Synthesis of graphene quantum dots doped by silver nanoparticle (ag NPs-GQDs) conductive nano ink
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized according to our previous work [47–49]. Then, first 2 mL of GQDs solution was heated at 60 °C in water bath, then 0.6 g graphite and 0.4 g PVP were added into prepared GQDs. The mixture was magnetically stirred until the PVP powder and graphite dissolving completely. Afterward 0.02 g AgNO3 that dissolved in 600 μl of water added into above solution and magnetically stirred for 5 min in order to ensure the efficient blending of GODs and Ag+. In the next step the prepared solution was incubated at 60 °C for 12 h in incubator. In the following the mixture solution magnetically stirred at 80 °C again. Then 0.12 g NaOH that dissolved in 400 μl added into above solution and magnetically stirred for 5 min. Afterwards prepared solution centrifuged for 30 min at 8000 rpm and 3 times washed with distilled water (DI) for removing extra reactant. In order to produce hybrid conductive ink for ink-jet printing, the attained Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink were dispersed into ethylene glycol, ethanol, DI water with volume ratio of 2:9:9 and sonicated for 30 min. After completion of the synthesis process, the produced ink was stored for 24 h before use at room temperature. Synthesis process of Ag NPs-GQDs conductive ink was shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conductivity study of Ag NPs-GQDs ink
ce:section.ce:para: For this purpose, various conductive patterns designed on the surface of photographic sheets via Ag NPs-GQDs pellet with different thicknesses. As depicted in Fig. 1 , different types of conducting lines (e.g., straight, spiral) and a dip LED with a rated voltage of 3.0 V the battery was fabricated on paper substrate (for spiral conductive track 15.0 V battery was used). One of the legs of LED (cathode) was connected to battery anode and so cathode surface of battery fixed to one side of conductive line. Once the anode leg of LED touched to other side of conductive line, the electronic circuit switched on and the LED was lighted up. Afterwards, the resistance of conductive lines was measured by ohmmeter. Also the conductivity measurements obtained 236 μs for Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink.

Prior to modification, the electrodes are successively polished with alumina, covered with a polished cloth. It is then immersed in a solution of water and acetone (ratio 1:1) for 1 h, after which immersed for 1 h in a mixture solution of 0.1 M nitric acids and 0.1 M HCL (ratio 1:1) then washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature. In purpose of electrodes modification, Ag NPs-GQDs nano-inks was poured in electrochemical cell and GCE was placed in cell. Then, electrodeposition was performed via cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique in 30 cycles and potential range of −1 to +1 V and scan rate of 100 mV/s. The results from cyclic voltammograms was shown in Fig. 2 reveals that successful electrodeposition of Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink on GCE surface.

After preparation of this interface, anti-CA-125 antibody mixed with EDC/NHS and incubated on Ag NPs-GQDs/GCE and placed in 4 °C for 1 h, then in purpose of blocking incubated anti-CA-125 antibody, Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/GCE immersed in BSA solution for 1 h, afterwards CA-125 antigen incubated on prepared Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/BSA/GCE and placed in 4 °C for 4 h. The fabrication procedure of electrochemical immunosensor for CA-125 detection was shown in Scheme 2 . It should be noted that after all steps of biological incubation, the obtained electrodes was rinsed with standard washing buffer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesis Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink and fabricated immunosensor
Characterization of synthesis Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink
ce:section.ce:para: The results obtained from FE-SEM imaging was shown in Fig. 3 which reveals excellent Ag NPs dispersion and distribution on the bulk solution sample of synthesized Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink. The following images approve that Ag NPs present in synthesized Ag NPs-GQDs nano ink as electrical signal amplifier and catalyst element. The formation and distribution of Ag NPs shown in different magnification in Fig. 3. Also Identification of synthesized Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink elements is also determined according to Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). According to Fig.4 , the quantitative results confirm the presence of carbon elements with high frequency and Ag NPs in bulk sample.

After modification of the GCE with Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink and immobilization of Ab, the electrochemical behavior of immunosensor was investigated before and after the incubation of the biological elements.

According to Fig. 5 , in this research, CV, DPV, SWV and EIS techniques were used. For the investigation of immunosensor performance, CVs was conducted in one cycle at a potential range of −1 to +1 V and a sweep rate of 100 mV/s (Fig. 5a). DVPs (Fig. 5b) and SWVs (Fig. 5c) were also conducted in the potential range of −1 to +1 V. in following, the EIS (Fig. 5d) was carried out at a fixed potential of −0.6 V and a frequency range of 1 Hz to 1 MHz All studies were performed in 1 mM Riboflavin solution as mediator.

According to CVs, the redox peak of Ag NPs-GQDs/GCE was increased after the modification of the GCE by the Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink. This result shows that the structure of the Ag NPs-GQDs nano ink matrix with its proper structure and conductivity is capable of effectively increasing the electron transfer rate of the riboflavin solution.

In the next step, after incubation of the mixture solution containing EDC/NHS as organic activators and anti-CA-125 antibody to the GC electrode surface modified by Ag NPs-GQDs ink, the peak currents was decreased. Blocking the electrode surface by anti-CA-125 antibody reduces the efficiency of the electron exchange from the mediator solution, and slow down the kinetics of electron transfer. It can be concluded that the anti-CA-125 antibody has been successfully immobilized at the modified GCE surface. Due to poor conductivity of antibody, the electron transfer occurs at a lower rate. Also, after CA-125 antigen incubation, the redox peak has been reduced again compared to the Ag NPs-GQDs matrix, which indicates the formation of the antibody-antigen complex successfully happened. The formation of this immune complex reduces conductivity and limits electron transport. The results obtained from the sensitive DPV and SWV techniques confirm the successful immobilization of biological components on the engineered surface. It is deduced from EIS results, including the Nyquist (d.1) and phase bode (d.2) plot analysis, which demonstrate the GCE surface is modified by an Ag NPs-GQDs nano-ink through electrochemical deposition and immobilization of antibody and antigen as biological elements.

Furthermore, according to the shape of the Nyquist and phase bode plots, it can be predicted that the equivalent circuit for obtaining set is equal to the Randles circuit. Which includes electrolytic solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, Warburg impedance and bilayer capacitor. Also, Phase bode shifts to 50 (rad/s).

The analytic performance of the Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/BSA/Ag immunosensor was studied by DPVs and SWVs techniques in different concentrations of CA-125 biomarker and the ability of the techniques was evaluated to determine the CA-125 biomarker.

Fig. 6a shows the DPVs results at different concentrations of CA-125 biomarkers in a standard sample with a sweep rate of 50 mV/s. Also Fig. 6b shows the calibration curve of various concentration, which shows the linear regression equation for concentrations ranging from 15 to 400 U/mL of standard sample and confirm the above-mentioned immunosensor and the sensitivity of the technique used. The linear regression equation obtained is as follows:
I
μA
= 0.0008
C
CA
125
+ 1.4249 ,
R 2
= 0.9852 .

As shown in Fig. 7 the study of different concentrations of CA-125 biomarker was evaluated by square wave voltammetry technique. According to the obtained results, the Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/BSA/Ag/GCE immunosensor by using square wave voltammetry technique has the ability to identify the CA-125 biomarker in the concentration range of 0.01 to 400 U/mL.

As well as in Fig. S5(c–e), the standard curves for high concentrations 15–400 U/mL (Curve c) and low concentration 0.5–0.01 U/mL (curves d & e) were plotted and the following linear regression equations were obtained:

Linear regression equation for high concentration 15–400 U/mL (Curve c):
I
μA
= 0.0015
C
CA
125
+ 2.5593 ,
R 2
= 0.9767 .

Linear regression equation for concentration range of 0.5–0.1 U/mL (Curve d):
I
μA
= 0.6143
C
CA
125
+ 2.0707 ,
R 2
= 0.9856 .

Linear regression equation for concentration range of 0.05–0.01 U/mL (Curve e):
I
μA
= 4.5102
C
CA
125
+ 1.7211 ,
R 2
= 0.8805 .

The lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) for the detection of the CA-125 biomarker was recorded as 0.01 U/mL which was compared with the previous reports (Table 1 ) [50–60]. The appropriate limit of quantification can be considered as a result of several factors involved in the preparation of the desired immunosensor, including excellent electrical conductivity, structural properties and high stability of the Ag NPs-GQDs matrix, appropriate surface area, optimal matrix reaction with a biological component for antibody immobilization and providing the feasibility of biological activity was used to identify the CA-125 antigens.

Furthermore, EIS study of CA-125 biomarker detection at various concentration in range of 15–400 U/mL was performed by using Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/BSA/Ag/GCE. According to Fig. S7 (see supporting information), the results of EIS were presented for concentrations range of 15–400 U/mL in the form of a Nyquist and phase bode plots. The shape of the Nyquist plot (Fig. S7 (see supporting information)) reflects the interaction between the electrode surface and the mediator electrolyte solution (riboflavin 1 mM) for electron transfer. From the phase bode (Fig. S7 (see supporting information)) study, it is also obtained that phase bode shifts are in the range of 15–50 (rad/s).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity study of engineered immunosensor in various concentration of CA-125 biomarker in human plasma sample
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on Fig. S8a (see supporting information), the CA-125 biomarker detection in human plasma sample was also evaluated by DPVs technique. The DPVs (a) show that the supplied immunosensor can be detect the biomarker in concentration range of 15–400 U/mL on the human plasma sample and the calibration curve (b) plotted to the linear regression equation is as follows:
I
μA
= 0.0003
C
CA
125
+ 0.8419 ,
R 2
= 0.996 .

Also, EIS study of the supplied immunosensor in various concentrations of the CA-125 biomarker in the human plasma sample in range of 400–0.01 U/mL was shown in Fig. S8(c–f (see supporting information)) at a fixed potential −0.6 V and a frequency range of 1 Hz to 1 MHz. As the results shown (Fig. S8c & d (see supporting information)), changes in concentration lead to alteration in the shape of the Nyquist plots which the supplied immunosensor has the ability to record changes in concentration range of 400–0.01 U/mL. Fig. S8 (e & f (see supporting information)), shows phase bode alteration in high concentrations range (plot e) from15 to 400 U/mL and phase bode shifts from 20 to 45 (rad/s). Also as seen in Fig. S8f (see supporting information), in low concentration (0.5–0.01 U/mL), phase bode shifts was occurred in the range of 10 to 55 (rad/s) which has wide shift range compare to high concentration. According to the shape of the Nyquist and phase bode plots, it can be predicted that the equivalent circuit for obtaining set is equal to the Randles circuit. Which includes electrolytic solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, Warburg impedance and bilayer capacitor.

In this research, a novel immunoassay based on Ag NPs-GQDs/anti-CA-125 antibody/BSA/CA-125 Ag was successfully designed to detection of the CA-125 biomarker in a human plasma sample. In summary, the Ag NPs-GQDs/Ab/BSA/Ag immunosensor was successfully designed to detection of the CA-125 biomarker in a human plasma sample. The supplied electrochemical immunosensor presents the proper ability to detect and determine the amount of CA-125 biomarker in low concentrations of CA-125 biomarker in human plasma sample. Under the optimized operating conditions, the low limit of quantitation (LLOQ) for the proposed immunosensor was recorded as 0.01 U/mL, which this evaluation was performed at highly linear range of 0.01–400 U/mL. Utilizing the electrochemical method might be improve performance of proposed immunosensor by enhanced accessible surface area for immobilizing a high amount of anti-CA-125 antibody, increasing electrical conductivity, boosting stability, catalytic properties and biocompatibility. Above all, synthesized Ag NPs-GQDs nano ink, in addition to having excellent electrical properties, it was proved as promising nano-material to use in types of flexible surfaces such as paper, skin surface due to its proper printable properties. These discussed features of the nano ink can be a fundamental evolution in the development of any fields of electrical industries especially in clinical diagnosis fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thanks Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research. Research reported in this publication was supported by National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD-under award number [972110]), Tehran, Iran, under award number. Also, we thanks Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.
